17: ei tis en Christo kaine ktisis ta archaia parelthen idou gegonen kaina (ei tis en Christō, kainē ktisis; ta archaia parēlthen, idou gegonen kaina): 'if anyone is in Christ, new creation (kainē ktisis); the old things have passed away, behold new things have come.' Kainē ktisis lacks a verb — it is an exclamation, not a predicate sentence: 'new creation!' The eschatological new creation of Isa 65:17-25 has erupted into the present in the person who is 'in Christ'. The aorist parēlthen (passed away) and perfect gegonen (have come and remain) frame the conversion as a realized eschatological event: the old age has definitively ended; the new age is permanently present.
21: ton me gnonta hamartian hyper hemon hamartian epoiesen (ton mē gnonta hamartian hyper hēmōn hamartian epoiēsen): 'him who knew no sin he made to be sin on our behalf.' The verse is the most compressed statement of substitutionary atonement in Paul. Hamartian epoiesen — 'made sin': not a sinner, not a sin offering (though that interpretation has ancient support; LXX uses hamartia for sin-offering in Lev 4-6), but 'sin' itself in its concentrated reality. The exchange: he became what we are (sin-bearers), so that in him we might become what he is (the righteousness of God). The prepositional phrase hyper hemon (on behalf of us) carries the weight of substitution throughout Paul's atonement-theology (cf. Gal 3:13; Rom 8:32).
1: Oikia tou skeynous: tent-house (skenos — tent, temporary dwelling). Oikodome ek theou, oikia achiropoietos aionios en tois ouranois: a building from God, not hand-made, eternal, heavenly — three qualities contrasting with the earthly tent. The eschatological body as permanent divine construction vs the temporary mortal body as fragile tent.
2: To oiketerion hemon to ex ouranou ependysasthai epipothountes: longing to put on over (ependyzomai — clothe over existing clothing) our heavenly dwelling. The eschatological body is not replacement but clothing-over: Paul seems to prefer transformation at the Parousia without passing through the naked intermediate state of death.
3: Ei ge kai endysamenoi ou gymnoi heurethesometha: if indeed, having put it on, we will not be found naked (gymnos). Nakedness = the disembodied state of death before resurrection. Paul's hope is not disembodiment (Greek philosophical idealism) but the heavenly body as new clothing. The ei ge conditional acknowledges the alternative: some may die before the Parousia.
4: Hoi ontes en to skenei stenaZomen baroymenoi: those in the tent groan, being burdened (bareo — same burden-verb as 1:8). Eph' ho ou thelomen ekdysasthai all' ependysasthai: not wishing to be unclothed (ekdyomai) but clothed over (ependyomai). Hina katapoth e to thneton hypo tes zoes: that the mortal may be swallowed up by life (reverse of 2:7 where grief 'swallows' the offender — the eschatological swallowing of death by life).
5: Ho de katergasamenos hemas eis auto touto theos, ho dous hemon ton arrhabona tou pneumatos: the one who fashioned/prepared us for this very purpose is God, who gave the Spirit as arrabon (pledge/down-payment — commercial-legal term). The Spirit as earnest of the eschatological body: the bodily resurrection hope is guaranteed by the Spirit's present indwelling.
6: Tharrountes oun pantote: being confident therefore always. Endemountes en to somati ekdemoumen apo tou Kyriou: being at home in the body (endemeuo) we are away from (ekdemeuo) the Lord. The spatial metaphor: physical embodiment is away-from-the-Lord — not sinful but the condition of the present age before the Parousia and full presence with Christ.
7: Dia pisteos gar peripatoumen, ou dia eidous: we walk (peripateo — conduct our lives) by faith (pistis, dispute level 2), not by sight (eidos — visible appearance). Condenses 4:18's epistemological reorientation: present life conducted by confident trust in the unseen, grounded in the resurrection-God's faithfulness established throughout ch 1.
8: Tharroymen de kai eudokoymen mallon ekdemesai ek tou somatos kai endemesai pros ton Kyrion: we prefer (eudokeo — take pleasure in) to be away from the body and at home with the Lord. This clarifies 5:6: the intermediate state (death before resurrection) is 'at home with the Lord' even if not the final goal. Paul's preference is personal presence with Christ in any bodily form.
9: Dio kai philotimoymetha eite endemoyntes eite ekdemoyntes euarestoi auto einai: we make it our ambition (philotimeomai — love of honor; here sanctified: Paul's goal is Christ's approval, not human recognition) to be well-pleasing to him. The Parousia judgment (v.10) grounds present moral effort regardless of the believer's state at any given moment.
10: Tous gar pantas hemas phanerothena dei emprosthen tou bematos tou Christou: we must all appear (phaneroo — be made manifest, become fully visible) before the bema (judgment seat) of Christ. Bema: the raised platform/tribunal for judgments — the Roman magistrate's seat (Paul stood before Gallio's bema in Acts 18:12; he projects all believers before Christ's). Ta dia tou somatos: the things done through the body — bodily actions have eschatological weight.
11: Eidos oun ton phobon tou Kyriou anthropous peithomen: knowing the fear of the Lord (phobos tou Kyriou) we persuade people. Paul's evangelism is grounded in reverent awe before Christ's bema. Theo de pephanerome tha: to God we have been made manifest — transparent accountability before God grounds the apostolic persuasion.
12: Ou palin heautous synistanomen hymin alla aphorm en didontes hymin kauchematos hyper hemon: not again commending ourselves but giving opportunity (aphorme — a launching pad) for boasting about us. Paul arms the Corinthians against the super-apostles by providing genuine knowledge of his apostolic character. The opponents boast in face/appearance (prosopon) rather than heart.
13: Eite gar exestemen, theo: if we are out of our minds (existemi — ecstatic, beside ourselves; probably referencing charismatic experiences or the intense letters), it is for God. Eite sophronoumen, hymin: if in our right mind, it is for you. The ecstasy-or-sobriety binary covers all apostolic modes: both states serve their purpose. Paul refuses to be categorized by the opponents' criteria.
14: He gar agape tou Christou synechei hemas: the love of Christ holds together/constrains (synecho — presses together, compels). Agape tou Christou: genitive ambiguous — Christ's love for us (subjective, favored by context) or our love for Christ. Krinantas touto hoti heis hyper panton apethanen: having concluded that one died for all, therefore all died. Substitutionary-participatory atonement: the one died as all, so all are reckoned as having died.
15: Kai hyper panton apethanen hina hoi zontes meketi heautois zosin alla to hyper auton apothanonti kai egerthen ti: he died for all so that the living might no longer live for themselves (heautois — self-oriented) but for him who died and was raised. The purpose of the atonement is the redirection of life-orientation. The resurrection is included: not just substitutionary death but the raised-and-living Christ who now claims the lives he redeemed.
16: Hoste hemeis apo tou nyn oudena oidamen kata sarka: from now on we know no one according to flesh (kata sarka). The death-resurrection event (vv.14-15) creates a new epistemological standpoint. Ei kai egnokamen kata sarka Christon, alla nyn ouketi ginoskomen: even if we once knew Christ according to flesh, now we know him thus no longer. Kata sarka: MKT 'from a worldly point of view'; the literal 'according to flesh' carries more weight — evaluation by human lineage, social status, religious credentials is replaced by the new-creation perspective.
18: Ta de panta ek tou theou tou katallagantos hemas heauto dia Christou: all things are from God who reconciled (katallasso — resolved the enmity; aorist) us to himself through Christ. Kai dontos hemin ten diakonian tes katalages: and gave us the ministry of reconciliation. The reconciliation is accomplished before the ministry of announcement begins — the apostle announces what God has already done.
19: Hos hoti theos en en Christo kosmon katallasson heauto: that God was in Christ reconciling the world to himself. Me logizomenos autois ta paraptomata auton: not counting their trespasses against them (logizomai — the ledger-verb of imputation). The reconciliation involves cancellation of the sin-debt — divine non-imputation as the mechanism of reconciliation.
20: Hyper Christou oun presbeuomen, hos tou theou parakalountos di' hemon: we are ambassadors (presbytero — official representatives with delegated authority) for Christ, as though God were making his appeal through us. The apostle is not a freelance preacher but an official envoy. Deometha hyper Christou: katallagete to theo: we beg on behalf of Christ — be reconciled to God. The urgency comes from knowing the bema (v.10) is coming.
